title: Families’ Rights and Responsibilities Act
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
plain_summary: This bill gives parents the basic right to make important decisions about their children. Parents can choose how to raise their kids, what kind of education they get, and what health care they receive. The government can only step in if there is a very serious reason and they use the gentlest way possible. If the government breaks these rules, parents can take them to court and might get their lawyer costs paid back if they win.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Families' Rights and Responsibilities Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Congressional Findings and Declaration of Purposes
sections.text: Congress believes the following facts are true. Parents have the main job and duty to take care of their children. Because parents have these duties, they also have a basic natural right to care for their children that existed before governments were formed. The job that parents do raising their children is very valuable and should be praised and protected by all levels of government. This right has been recognized throughout Western history. It means parents have the duty to love, care for, raise, and protect their children. This right gives parents the power to decide how their children are raised, educated, and receive health care based on what they believe is right. Parents get to make decisions about their children's upbringing, education, and health care according to their own beliefs and religion. Parents are the main decision makers for their children until the children become adults. The Supreme Court has always recognized that parents have the main role in caring for children. The Court has said many important things about this. The child does not belong to the government. The people who raise the child have the right and duty to prepare the child for grown-up responsibilities. It is the natural duty of parents to give their children education that fits their place in life. A parent's interest in being with their child and taking care of them deserves special respect from the Court. The history and culture of Western civilization shows a strong tradition of parents caring about raising their children. This main role of parents in raising children is now clearly established as a lasting American tradition. Our legal system has always reflected Western ideas about the family as a unit where parents have broad authority over their minor children. The relationship between parent and child is protected by the Constitution. The liberty specially protected by the Due Process Clause includes the right to direct the education and upbringing of one's children. Parents have a basic right to make decisions about the care, custody, and control of their children. The Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment protects this basic right. The Due Process Clause does not let a state interfere with parents' basic right to make child-rearing decisions just because a state judge thinks a better decision could be made. Some federal court decisions have failed to recognize parents' basic rights. This has led to the wrong legal test being used when reviewing government actions that hurt parental rights. Government agencies have increasingly interfered with parents' proper decisions in situations that do not involve abuse or neglect. Instead, agencies simply disagree with parenting choices based on decent and honorable religious or philosophical beliefs. When government gets involved in parenting, it should focus on supporting the parent's role as the child's main teacher. Government should support, not replace, parents' rights and responsibilities. Government should not interfere with parents' decisions and actions without a very strong reason. Courts use a strict scrutiny test to evaluate cases about basic rights. This is the correct legal standard for reviewing government actions that interfere with parents' right to direct their children's upbringing, education, and health care. This test properly balances the interests of parents, children, and government. The goals of this law are to protect parents' right to direct their children's upbringing as a basic right. While protecting parents' rights, this law also recognizes that rights come with responsibilities. Parents have the responsibility to educate, care for, and raise their children as the Supreme Court has said in several cases. Parents have the high duty to recognize and prepare their children for grown-up responsibilities as the Supreme Court said in another case.

sections.title: Section 3 Definitions
sections.text: In this law, these words mean the following. Government means any branch, department, agency, office, and official of the United States. It also includes anyone acting under the authority of law for the United States, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, and all territories and possessions of the United States. Parent means a biological parent of a child, an adoptive parent of a child, or a person who has been given the exclusive right and authority over a child's welfare under state law. Child means a person who is not yet eighteen years old. Substantial burden means any action that directly or indirectly limits, stops, reduces, or denies parents' right to direct their child's upbringing, education, and health care. It also means forcing any action that goes against parents' right to direct their child's upbringing, education, and health care. This includes withholding benefits, imposing criminal, civil, or administrative penalties or damages, or excluding people from government programs.

sections.title: Section 4 Protection of Parental Rights
sections.text: The freedom of parents to direct their children's upbringing, education, and health care is a basic right. Government cannot substantially burden this basic right of parents without proving that the interference is required by a very important government interest of the highest level. The government must show this interest applies to both the parent and child. The government must also use the least restrictive way possible to advance that very important government interest. The basic rights protected include, but are not limited to, these rights and responsibilities. The right to direct the child's education. The right to direct the moral or religious upbringing of the child. The right to access and review all medical records of the child and to make and consent to all physical and mental health care decisions for the child. Unless legally given up or legally ended, parents have permanent rights that are more complete than those listed in this section. This law does not list all rights of parents. This law does not prevent or stop claims or remedies supporting parental rights that are available under any other federal law, state law, the United States Constitution, or a state constitution. This section does not apply to a parental action or decision that would cause serious physical injury to the child or that would end the child's life. Any parent may raise a violation of this law as a claim or defense in an action in federal or state court or before an administrative court. Parents can get appropriate relief against a government. The right to make a claim or defense under this section follows the general rules about who can sue under article three of the Constitution.

sections.title: Section 5 Attorney Fees
sections.text: The law about attorney fees in court cases is changed to include the Families' Rights and Responsibilities Act before the Civil Rights Act of nineteen sixty four. The law about attorney fees in administrative cases is changed. It now covers any legal proceeding under the Religious Freedom Restoration Act of nineteen ninety three or the Families' Rights and Responsibilities Act.

sections.title: Section 6 Applicability
sections.text: This law applies to each federal law and how any such law is put into practice. This includes laws that are written in statutes or come from other sources. It applies whether the laws were adopted before or after this law was passed. The protections of parents' basic right to direct their children's upbringing, education, and health care given by this law are in addition to protections provided under federal law, state law, and state and federal constitutions. This law should be understood to give broad protection to parents' basic right to direct their children's upbringing, education, and health care. Nothing in this law should be understood to allow any government to burden parents' basic right to direct their children's upbringing, education, and health care. Federal laws passed after this law is enacted are subject to this law. The only exception is if such a law specifically excludes this application by mentioning this law by name.
